topic: Introduction to Nephrology / Investigations of the Renal System

cards.q: Define paediatric nephrology.
cards.a: The branch of paediatrics responsible for the diagnosis, evaluation and management of the disorders of the kidneys and urinary tract in children.

cards.q: What five things should a nephrologist be able to do?
cards.a: Evaluate and diagnose renal pathology; institute appropriate treatment; monitor growth and development in children with kidney disorders; provide renal replacement therapy; and educate and counsel families on reno-prevention and long term care.

cards.q: Describe the position and covering of the kidneys.
cards.a: Paired, bean-shaped, retroperitoneal organs located between T12 and L3 vertebrae, with the left kidney more superior than the right; covered by the renal fascia — Gerota's fascia.

cards.q: State the weight and dimensions of the kidneys.
cards.a: About 150 g in males and 135 g in females; 10–12 cm in length, 5–7 cm in width, and 2–3 cm in thickness.

cards.q: What proportion of cardiac output do the kidneys receive, and at what level do the renal arteries arise?
cards.a: About 20% of cardiac output, from the paired renal arteries at the level of L2.

cards.q: Describe the innervation of the kidney.
cards.a: Sympathetic innervation arises from the spinal cord at T8–L1, and parasympathetic from the vagus nerves; these cause vasoconstriction and vasodilation respectively.

cards.q: List the components of the nephron in order.
cards.a: The renal corpuscle (glomerulus and Bowman's capsule); the PCT in the renal cortex; the descending loop of Henle from cortex to medulla; the ascending limb; the distal convoluted tubule in the cortex; and the collecting duct, from cortex through medulla to renal pelvis.

cards.q: List the seven functions of the kidneys.
cards.a: Filtration and excretion of metabolic waste products; fluid and electrolyte balance; blood pressure regulation; endocrine function; acid-base homeostasis; regulation of red blood cell production; and calcium and bone metabolism.

cards.q: List the common renal disorders.
cards.a: CAKUT, UTI, nephrotic syndrome, AGN, AKI, CKD, vesicoureteric reflux, haemolytic uraemic syndrome, and renal tubular disorders.

cards.q: List the risk factors for renal disorders.
cards.a: Drug toxicity, family history/genetics, obesity, hypertension, prematurity, diabetes, and autoimmune disorders.

cards.q: List the clinical features of renal disorders.
cards.a: Oliguria/anuria, oedema, haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, electrolyte imbalance, and lower abdominal pain.

cards.q: State the rationale for investigations in nephrology.
cards.a: To confirm a clinical diagnosis; to assess structural and functional integrity of the kidneys and urinary tract; to assess the severity and extent of the disease; for prognostication; and to exclude other co-morbidities.

cards.q: What four considerations are important when choosing an investigation?
cards.a: Diagnostic value, availability, cost and sustainability.

cards.q: List the seven classes of investigation in nephrology.
cards.a: Urine examinations; blood investigations; GFR estimation; assessment of tubular function; imaging; biopsy; and bladder function assessment.

cards.q: Why must urine samples be examined freshly, and how may they be stored?
cards.a: To avoid disintegration of cellular elements and bacterial overgrowth; urine can be stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

cards.q: List the methods of urine collection.
cards.a: MSU (with a long bladder time); clean catch method; bagging; suprapubic aspiration (SPA); catheterization; and use of a mid-stream urinary collector.

cards.q: What is the drawback of bagging, and what is its value?
cards.a: It is associated with a high risk of contamination and the bag must be removed as soon as urine is passed; it is important for its negative predictive value.

cards.q: Which method of urine collection is most reliable, and in whom is it useful?
cards.a: Suprapubic aspiration — useful in children less than 2 years.

cards.q: State the significant colony counts for SPA, CSU and MSU.
cards.a: SPA — any growth is significant, because bladder urine is supposed to be sterile. CSU — ≥ 10⁴ CFU/mL. MSU — ≥ 10⁵ CFU/mL.

cards.q: What growth indicates UTI on catheterization in a girl, or midstream clean-void in a circumcised boy?
cards.a: Usually > 50,000 CFU/mL of a single urinary pathogen; however UTI may be present with 10,000–50,000 CFU/mL of a single organism.

cards.q: What growth indicates UTI on midstream clean-void collection in a girl or uncircumcised boy?
cards.a: > 100,000 CFU/mL of a single urinary pathogen in a symptomatic patient, with pyuria usually present; UTI may be present with 10,000–50,000 CFU/mL.

cards.q: In an asymptomatic patient, how is bacterial growth interpreted?
cards.a: Growth is usually > 100,000 CFU/mL of the same organism on different days; and if pyuria is absent, the result probably indicates colonization rather than infection.

cards.q: Which patients are most likely to have a UTI with low colony counts, and why?
cards.a: Patients with urinary frequency — because decreased bladder incubation time gives bacteria less opportunity to proliferate.

cards.q: List the parameters on a routine urine dipstick.
cards.a: Leukocyte, nitrite, protein, pH, blood, specific gravity, ketone, bilirubin, glucose, ascorbic acid, and urobilinogen.

cards.q: State the principle of the urine dipstick.
cards.a: Urine constituents react with a chemical impregnated in the specific pad on the strip, producing a colour change; the intensity of colour gives a qualitative grading read off the chart on the container.

cards.q: What determines the quality of a dipstick result?
cards.a: Strict adherence to the manufacturer's instructions.

cards.q: State the estimated urine protein concentration for each dipstick grade.
cards.a: Trace < 30 mg/dL; + 30 mg/dL; 2+ 100 mg/dL; 3+ 300 mg/dL; 4+ > 1000 mg/dL.

cards.q: List the causes of false negative proteinuria.
cards.a: Dilute or very acidic urine, and proteinuria from other proteins.

cards.q: List the causes of false positive proteinuria.
cards.a: Concentrated urine, alkaline urine, gross haematuria, delay in reading, and urine contamination with vaginal secretions or antiseptic.

cards.q: What other urine examinations are performed?
cards.a: Urine macroscopy (appearance, colour change, odour); urine microscopy for RBC, WBC, casts and crystals; urine culture and sensitivity; and spot urine protein-creatinine ratio.

cards.q: List the blood tests used in nephrology.
cards.a: Serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine, calcium, phosphate, magnesium and vitamin D assay; FBC, MP and ESR; arterial blood gases; and immunologic tests.

cards.q: List the immunologic tests used in nephrology and what they indicate.
cards.a: C3, C4, CH50; ASO titre; anti-DNAse B; IgG and IgA for SLE and JRA; ANCA for Wegener granulomatosis and crescentic glomerulonephritis; and hormonal profile — T3, T4, TSH.

cards.q: Why is GFR estimated?
cards.a: To assess renal function, detect renal disease, assess severity, and for prognostication.

cards.q: Name the commonly used markers for GFR estimation.
cards.a: Inulin, polyfructose, Cr-EDTA, creatinine, cystatin C, and urea.

cards.q: State the three properties of an ideal marker for GFR estimation.
cards.a: It should be freely filtered at the glomerulus; should achieve a stable plasma concentration; and should not be reabsorbed, secreted or metabolized by the kidney.

cards.q: Why is creatinine not an ideal marker for GFR?
cards.a: Because it is secreted in the proximal tubule, and its concentration is influenced by age, sex, height and dietary protein.

cards.q: How is creatinine clearance measured?
cards.a: Using a timed urine collection — 24 hours, or 6–12 hours.

cards.q: Why is urea not an ideal marker for GFR?
cards.a: Because it is reabsorbed by the renal tubules, and its plasma level is affected by dietary protein intake and state of hydration.

cards.q: State the Schwartz formula.
cards.a: GFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) = 0.413 × height (cm) / serum creatinine (mg/dL).

cards.q: Why is GFR expressed per 1.73 m²?
cards.a: Because 1.73 m² is the average body surface area of an ideal adult, allowing comparison across body sizes.

cards.q: State the formula for fractional excretion of sodium.
cards.a: FENa (%) = quantity of Na excreted / quantity of Na filtered × 100 = (UNa × Pcr) / (PNa × Ucr) × 100.

cards.q: What determines urinary sodium excretion normally, and how is FENa interpreted?
cards.a: Extracellular fluid volume normally determines urinary sodium excretion; FENa is ≤ 1 in normovolaemia and pre-renal AKI, and > 1 in impaired tubular transport such as ATN.

cards.q: Name the tests of renal tubular function other than fractional excretion.
cards.a: Concentrating ability test (osmolality, pH); water deprivation test; diluting ability test; and urine acidification test.

cards.q: What is the water deprivation test used to differentiate?
cards.a: Central diabetes insipidus from nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

cards.q: What is the diluting ability test used for?
cards.a: Assessment of unexplained hyponatraemia.

cards.q: What does the urine acidification test involve?
cards.a: Serial urine pH, bicarbonate excretion, titratable acid, NH₃ excretion, and the ammonium chloride challenge test.

cards.q: How does ultrasonography work?
cards.a: It uses sound waves beyond human auditory capability (> 20 kHz); the transducer produces the sound wave and also receives the reflected wave, converting the echoes to electrical impulses which are displayed as images on the monitor.

cards.q: List the uses of renal ultrasonography.
cards.a: Investigating causes of urinary tract infections; checking for causes of urinary tract obstruction; diagnosing kidney stones, masses, cysts and tumours; evaluating congenital urinary tract abnormality; assessing kidney injury from trauma; and monitoring kidney transplant status or vascular issues.

cards.q: List the merits of ultrasonography.
cards.a: No ionizing radiation; real time images; readily available; relatively cheap; and portable, point-of-care option.

cards.q: List the demerits of ultrasonography.
cards.a: Cannot see through compact bone (posterior acoustic shadow); gas distorts the image; user dependence; and depth limitation.

cards.q: List the radiographic imaging modalities in nephrology.
cards.a: Plain film; intravenous urography; voiding cysto-urethrography; antegrade and retrograde pyelography; and renal arteriography.

cards.q: What is a plain film used for in nephrology?
cards.a: Diagnosis of nephrocalcinosis and radio-opaque stones in the pelvis, ureters and bladder; diagnosis of congenital spinal abnormalities with urinary symptom manifestation; and before injecting contrast material for intravenous urography. It is declining with the advent of USS.

cards.q: What is intravenous urography used for?
cards.a: To demonstrate anatomy of the urinary tract, giving details of the calyces and ureters; in the diagnosis of occult duplex kidney, malrotation, site of urinary tract obstruction and calculi; and it can detect renal scars.

cards.q: List the demerits of intravenous urography.
cards.a: High radiation exposure; low sensitivity for renal scar detection; potential reaction to contrast; and it is not used in neonates, young infants, or patients with renal failure.

cards.q: What is voiding cysto-urethrography, and what is it the gold standard for?
cards.a: The definitive method for demonstrating the lower urinary tract; it is the gold standard for diagnosing and grading VUR and PUV obstruction.

cards.q: How is a VCUG performed?
cards.a: Contrast is injected into the bladder to the upper tract, and films are taken during instillation, voiding and post-voiding.

cards.q: List the indications for VCUG.
cards.a: Suspected ureteric dilatation; small contracted kidneys; renal failure of indeterminate cause; terminal haematuria; voiding difficulties; and a thick walled bladder seen by ultrasound.

cards.q: What are the demerits of VCUG?
cards.a: High dose of radiation, and risk of UTI.

cards.q: Which radioisotopes are used in dynamic renography, and what are its uses?
cards.a: Technetium-labelled DTPA (Tc-DTPA) and Tc-MAG3; used for determining differential renal function, indirect voiding cystography, evaluation of post-transplantation state, and evaluating surgical treatment involving the kidneys.

cards.q: What are the merits and demerits of dynamic renography?
cards.a: It has a low dose of radiation; demerits are poor visualization of the renal cortex, and it cannot be used to evaluate renal size or morphology.

cards.q: List the uses of the static renal scan.
cards.a: In renovascular diseases and dribbling urinary incontinence; location of ectopic or absent kidney; detection of renal scars; the acute phase of pyelonephritis to demonstrate parenchymal involvement; and to detect focal parenchymal abnormalities.

cards.q: What are the drawbacks of the static renal scan?
cards.a: It is time-consuming, and does not give information on the function of the urinary tract.

cards.q: What are the indications and merits of CT in nephrology?
cards.a: Indications are similar to those of ultrasound; it gives clearer resolution, can detect fine calcifications within the renal parenchyma and collecting system, and with contrast enhancement can evaluate masses in the pelvis or bladder such as Wilms tumour. It is expensive and carries a risk of considerable radiation.

cards.q: What are the uses and drawbacks of MRI in nephrology?
cards.a: For detailed evaluation of complex urinary system disorders; it gives excellent pelvic floor details and is good for renal masses and cystic lesions, with no radiation exposure — but heavy sedation is needed.

cards.q: Define renal biopsy and list its types.
cards.a: The process of obtaining tissue from the kidney for histopathological evaluation; types are percutaneous, transvenous or transjugular, laparoscopic, and open surgical — the percutaneous approach is preferred by the paediatric nephrologist.

cards.q: Name the renal biopsy needles.
cards.a: Vim-Silverman needle; manual Tru-cut biopsy needle; semi-automatic Tru-cut biopsy needle; and the Tru-cut spring-loaded automatic biopsy system.

cards.q: List the general indications for renal biopsy.
cards.a: Establishment of the exact diagnosis; as an aid to determine the nature of recommended therapy; to help decide when treatment is futile; to ascertain the degree of active (potentially reversible) and chronic (irreversible) changes; follow up of treatment or disease; and research.

cards.q: What limits the accuracy of prognostication from a renal biopsy?
cards.a: Sample size, especially in focal lesions — biopsies with fewer than 5 glomeruli may not be accurate.

cards.q: State the indications for biopsy in nephrotic syndrome.
cards.a: Age less than 1 or greater than 7 years; steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome; and those with evidence of nephritis — hypertension, haematuria, low C3, or decreased renal function persisting in spite of volume correction.

cards.q: State the indications for biopsy in acute glomerulonephritis.
cards.a: A course not typical of PSAGN; HSP or SLE; assessment of severity of injury to guide therapy and prognosis; differentiation of specific types of proliferative lesions such as MPGN and C3 glomerulopathies including dense deposit disease; and recurrent persistent haematuria or proteinuria.

cards.q: State the indications for biopsy in AKI.
cards.a: Unexplained AKI when pre- and post-renal causes are excluded; AKI associated with nephritis, nephrotic syndrome, vasculitis or systemic disease; persisting ATN when the cause remains uncertain after complete evaluation; and suspected RPGN.

cards.q: Which serological and immunofluorescence tests accompany biopsy in suspected RPGN?
cards.a: Serology — anti-GBM antibody and ANCA as screening tests for necrotizing vasculitides; immunofluorescence — p-ANCA, c-ANCA, MPO or PR3.

cards.q: State the indications for biopsy in CKD.
cards.a: When kidneys are not shrunken; diagnosis of primary disease; assessment of severity of morphologic lesions; determination of risk of recurrence in eventual renal transplant; and suitability of deceased versus living-related donor transplantation.

cards.q: State the indications for biopsy in systemic disease and renal transplant.
cards.a: Systemic disease — to assess severity of renal disease e.g. SLE and atypical HUS, and in diabetics with a course atypical of diabetic nephropathy. Transplant — acute rejection, recurrence of kidney disease, calcineurin toxicity, some infections, and chronic allograft rejection.

cards.q: List the conditions NOT requiring renal biopsy.
cards.a: Isolated glomerular haematuria (microscopic); isolated non-nephrotic haematuria; childhood nephrotic syndrome aged over 1 year and under 7 years; and frequently relapsing nephrotic syndrome.

cards.q: State the transport medium for each biopsy examination.
cards.a: Light microscopy — 10% buffered formalin; immunofluorescence — Michel's transport media; electron microscopy — glutaraldehyde.

cards.q: What is the absolute contraindication to renal biopsy?
cards.a: Uncorrectable bleeding diathesis.

cards.q: List the other contraindications to renal biopsy.
cards.a: Uncontrolled hypertension; active renal or peri-renal infection; hydronephrosis; obesity; ascites; small shrunken kidneys; tumours, large cysts, abscesses or pyelonephritis; solitary, ectopic or horseshoe kidney; uncooperative patient; skin infection over the biopsy site; and when a skilled operator or appropriate pathology support is not available.

cards.q: How is a patient prepared for renal biopsy?
cards.a: Admit at least the day before; take a history of bleeding disorders; ensure the patient is not on warfarin, any anticoagulant or antithrombotic agent, and has had no aspirin or other NSAID for at least 7 days; control hypertension before biopsy; and inform the patient and parents, explain risks and obtain consent.

cards.q: State the laboratory requirements before renal biopsy.
cards.a: PCV > 24–30%; platelet count at least 100,000/mm³; INR < 1.2; APTT < 2× control or < 40 seconds; plus E&U and creatinine, abdominal USS KUB, urinalysis, urine m/c/s, and group and cross-match of 1 unit of blood.

cards.q: Why is an abdominal USS KUB done before renal biopsy?
cards.a: To assess whether the patient has two kidneys, and to assess kidney sizes.

cards.q: How long after haemodialysis should a renal biopsy be performed?
cards.a: After at least 24 hours from the last dialysis session.

cards.q: What is given for patients with a prolonged bleeding time before biopsy?
cards.a: IV desmopressin 0.3 µg/kg 30 minutes prior, or intranasal DDAVP 2–4 µg/kg 2 hours before the procedure.

cards.q: State the premedication for renal biopsy.
cards.a: NPO 4 hours before, commencing 5% dextrose water at maintenance; local anaesthesia and sedation with 1–2 doses of midazolam (0.1 mg/kg) and ketamine 0.5–1.0 mg/kg, with an anaesthetist; ± IV atropine 0.01 mg/kg 1–2 minutes after midazolam.

cards.q: What positions are used for renal biopsy?
cards.a: Prone for biopsy of the native kidney; supine for a graft kidney; and lateral decubitus in conditions such as abdominal distension and ascites.

cards.q: Which kidney is usually biopsied, and from which part?
cards.a: The left kidney is usually biopsied; the lower pole is identified under real-time ultrasound, and the sample is obtained from the renal cortex.

cards.q: Describe the technique of percutaneous renal biopsy.
cards.a: Clean the skin with methylated spirit and povidone iodine; give local anaesthesia with 1% lignocaine under ultrasound guidance; make a nick on the skin; advance the biopsy needle under real-time ultrasound into the cortex; using a 16G or 18G needle; 2–3 passes; and the core can be examined for the presence of glomeruli.

cards.q: State the post-biopsy monitoring schedule.
cards.a: Vital signs — BP, heart rate, respiratory rate and temperature — quarter-hourly for 1 hour, half-hourly for 2 hours, hourly for 4 hours, then 4-hourly for 24 hours.

cards.q: What other post-biopsy instructions apply?
cards.a: Do a post-biopsy clinical evaluation; keep a urine rack; give IV 10% dextrose at maintenance; monitor fluid input and output; allow the patient to eat once fully conscious; and watch out for gross haematuria, abdominal pain or flank pain.

cards.q: List the complications of renal biopsy.
cards.a: Significant haematuria; anaemia and hypotension; bowel or solid organ perforation or laceration; loss of a kidney; death; AV malformation; hypertension; and persistent loin pain.

cards.q: Define urodynamic studies and state when they are useful.
cards.a: Investigations done to evaluate the anatomy and physiology of the lower urinary tract, especially the filling and emptying of the bladder and urethra; useful in neurogenic bladder, urinary incontinence, voiding dysfunction, VUR, and anorectal malformations.

cards.q: Name the non-invasive and invasive urodynamic studies.
cards.a: Non-invasive — uroflow study/uroflowmetry, which measures urine speed and volume, and post-void residual urine estimation. Invasive — cystometry studies, pressure flow study, and electromyography.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is the left kidney usually chosen for biopsy?
cards.a: Because the liver pushes the right kidney downward, so the left kidney lies more superior and is easier and safer to reach.

cards.q: OSPE — Why must the biopsy sample come from the renal cortex?
cards.a: Because the glomeruli are located in the cortex; a medullary sample contains no glomeruli and is diagnostically useless.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is the lower pole targeted in renal biopsy?
cards.a: Because it is furthest from the renal hilum where the large vessels enter, reducing the risk of major vascular injury.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is the prone position used for native renal biopsy?
cards.a: Because the kidneys are retroperitoneal and are therefore approached from the back; a graft kidney lies in the iliac fossa anteriorly, so the supine position is used.

cards.q: OSPE — Why must aspirin be stopped 7 days before renal biopsy?
cards.a: Because aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelets, which live 7–10 days; the entire affected platelet population must be replaced.

cards.q: OSPE — Why are so many biopsy contraindications related to bleeding?
cards.a: Because the kidneys receive about 20% of cardiac output, making them one of the most vascular organs in the body.

cards.q: OSPE — Why are small shrunken kidneys a contraindication to biopsy?
cards.a: Because they indicate end-stage irreversible disease, so the biopsy carries all the risk without changing management.

cards.q: OSPE — A child has nephrotic syndrome aged 4 with normal BP, no haematuria and normal C3. Is biopsy indicated?
cards.a: No — childhood nephrotic syndrome aged over 1 and under 7 years without evidence of nephritis does not require biopsy; treat with steroids and observe the response.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the difference between frequently relapsing and steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome regarding biopsy?
cards.a: Frequently relapsing nephrotic syndrome does NOT require biopsy, because it still responds to steroids; steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome DOES require biopsy, because it never responded.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does the phrase 'persisting in spite of volume correction' matter in nephrotic syndrome?
cards.a: Because a nephrotic child is intravascularly depleted despite being oedematous, which raises creatinine pre-renally; only if the impairment persists after volume correction does it indicate intrinsic renal damage.

cards.q: OSPE — A child has oliguria and rising creatinine after gastroenteritis. Which test distinguishes pre-renal AKI from ATN?
cards.a: Fractional excretion of sodium — FENa ≤ 1 indicates pre-renal AKI with working tubules; FENa > 1 indicates ATN with impaired tubular transport.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is a low FENa reassuring?
cards.a: Because it shows the tubules are actively reabsorbing sodium — they are alive and responding correctly to volume depletion elsewhere in the body.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does distinguishing pre-renal AKI from ATN change management?
cards.a: Pre-renal AKI responds to volume replacement; in ATN, aggressive fluid loading will not restore function and will cause fluid overload, pulmonary oedema and hypertension.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does urea rise disproportionately in pre-renal AKI?
cards.a: Because urea is reabsorbed by the renal tubules along with water, and in volume depletion the kidney reabsorbs water avidly.

cards.q: OSPE — Ultrasound shows a dilated ureter and thick-walled bladder in a boy with recurrent febrile UTI. Which investigation is indicated?
cards.a: Voiding cysto-urethrography — the gold standard for diagnosing and grading VUR and PUV obstruction; suspected ureteric dilatation and a thick-walled bladder seen on US are both listed indications.

cards.q: OSPE — Why must films be taken during voiding in a VCUG?
cards.a: Because bladder pressure is highest during voiding, so reflux is most likely to be demonstrated then; a study without voiding films can miss the diagnosis.

cards.q: OSPE — What complication is being prevented by diagnosing VUR early?
cards.a: Renal scarring from recurrent pyelonephritis, which leads to hypertension and chronic kidney disease.

cards.q: OSPE — Which investigation best detects renal scars, and why?
cards.a: The static renal scan — the tracer is taken up only by functioning tubular cells, so a scar appears as a cold defect. IVU has low sensitivity for renal scar detection.

cards.q: OSPE — Which investigation determines differential renal function, and why does it matter?
cards.a: Dynamic renography with Tc-DTPA or Tc-MAG3 — it shows how much each kidney contributes, which determines whether an obstructed kidney should be repaired or removed.

cards.q: OSPE — Why can ultrasound not be used to assess renal function?
cards.a: Because it shows structure only; two normal-looking kidneys may be in complete failure.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is IVU contraindicated in renal failure?
cards.a: Because the test depends on the kidney excreting the contrast; a failing kidney gives a poor image and is exposed to a nephrotoxic contrast load.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does a stone cast a shadow on ultrasound?
cards.a: Because it reflects almost all the ultrasound beam, so nothing passes behind it — the same posterior acoustic shadow produced by bone.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does chronic kidney disease cause anaemia?
cards.a: Because the kidney produces erythropoietin, which stimulates red cell production in the bone marrow; its loss is a hormone deficiency, not blood loss.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does chronic kidney disease cause bone disease?
cards.a: Because the kidney performs the final hydroxylation that activates vitamin D; without it, calcium is not absorbed, blood calcium falls, PTH rises and calcium is drawn from bone.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must growth and development be monitored in a child with kidney disease?
cards.a: Because chronic acidosis, anaemia and impaired calcium and bone metabolism all impair growth — the kidney's endocrine functions directly affect it.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A dipstick shows no protein in a child with suspected nephrotic syndrome. What might explain a false negative?
cards.a: Dilute urine or very acidic urine, or proteinuria consisting of proteins other than albumin, which the strip mainly detects.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is a bag urine specimen of limited value in a febrile infant?
cards.a: Because of the high risk of contamination; it is useful mainly for its negative predictive value — a negative result is reassuring, a positive one is unreliable.
